summary:
Solon's Reforms (594 BC) were the comprehensive legal and social restructuring of Athens carried out by the poet-statesman Solon, who was elected archon and given extraordinary powers to address a crisis in which debt slavery threatened to reduce most of the Athenian free population to bondage. His seisachtheia ('shaking off of burdens') cancelled all debts secured by the person of the debtor, freed those enslaved for debt, and prohibited future debt slavery — one of the most sweeping financial reforms in the ancient world.

Beyond debt relief, Solon reorganized Athenian society by property class (pentacosiomedimni, hippeis, zeugitai, thetes) — access to offices determined by wealth rather than birth, weakening aristocratic exclusivity. He created the Council of 400 (boule) as a deliberative body preparing business for the assembly, expanded the competence of popular juries (heliaia), and reformed weights and measures to facilitate trade. His laws were inscribed on rotating wooden tablets (axones) and displayed publicly for all to consult. Solon famously refused to become tyrant when offered the chance, and left Athens for ten years to prevent pressure to modify his laws — later held up as the model of the civic-minded lawgiver who places community above personal power. His reforms did not prevent the tyranny of [[event:1991|Peisistratos Tyranny Athens]] but created the constitutional framework that [[event:1934|Cleisthenes' Democratic Reforms]] would later complete.
